q: what is a datom and what fields does it contain
gold_answer: A datom is the atomic append-only unit of truth in cortex. Each datom carries a tx (ULID transaction id), actor, op (assert/retract), subject, attribute, value, checksum, and invocation id. Datoms are sealed before append and replayed to rebuild derived state.
rephrasings: define the datom record structure
which fields make up a single datom

q: how is base activation B(e) calculated
gold_answer: B(e) = ln(sum over past reinforcements of (age_seconds ^ -decay_exponent)). Decay exponent defaults to 0.5 and is controlled by retrieval.activation.decay_exponent.
rephrasings: what is the formula for base level activation
how does cortex compute the base-level component of ACT-R activation

q: what are the four weights in ACT-R composite activation
gold_answer: The composite activation is a weighted sum of base-level (0.3), PPR (0.3), similarity (0.3), and importance (0.1). Weights live under retrieval.activation.weights in config.
rephrasings: list the components of the composite activation score
how is final score composed from base ppr similarity importance

q: what are the recall pipeline steps from query to results
gold_answer: concept extraction -> seed resolution -> Personalized PageRank -> entry load -> ACT-R activation rerank -> trail/community context attachment. Every surfaced entry is reinforced via an activation-update datom appended after rendering.
rephrasings: walk me through the default recall flow
what are the stages of cortex recall

q: how does the append-only log detect corruption
gold_answer: Each datom is sealed with a checksum. On startup the tail window (log.tail_validation_window_bytes) is validated; corrupt suffixes are truncated and self-heal replays missing datoms.
rephrasings: how does cortex verify log segment integrity
what guarantees log tail validity after crash

q: what does principled forgetting mean in cortex
gold_answer: Entries whose activation falls below retrieval.forgetting.visibility_threshold (0.05) are hidden from recall but not deleted. Explicit control via pin/unpin/evict. The log stays intact so state can be rebuilt.
rephrasings: explain cortex forgetting semantics
how does visibility threshold affect recall

q: what is the role of trails in cortex
gold_answer: A trail is a bracketed session of episodic observations bound by trail begin / trail end. Observations inside a trail share CORTEX_TRAIL_ID and are summarized at end.
rephrasings: how do trail begin and trail end work
what is a cortex trail for

q: what storage backends does cortex use
gold_answer: Neo4j (graph), Weaviate (vectors), Ollama (embeddings and concept extraction). The append-only datom log on disk is the authoritative source of truth; the backends are derived state.
rephrasings: which databases back cortex
what services does cortex depend on at runtime

q: what is semantic frame consolidation
gold_answer: Reflection promotes clusters of episodic observations into semantic frames when they meet min_cluster_size, min_distinct_timestamps, avg_pairwise_cosine_floor, and MDL compression ratio thresholds.
rephrasings: how does reflection promote clusters into frames
what are the cluster promotion thresholds

q: what does self-heal do on startup
gold_answer: Self-heal replays datoms from the log that are missing from the derived backends (Neo4j, Weaviate) so that rebuilt state matches the log after a crash or partial failure.
rephrasings: how does cortex recover from a crash
what happens at cortex startup to reconcile state

q: where is retrieval.ppr.damping defined and what is its default
gold_answer: retrieval.ppr.damping defaults to 0.85. It lives in the Retrieval.PPR section of config and is passed to the PPRRunner at recall time.
rephrasings: what is the default PPR damping factor

q: what is ingest.module_size_limit_bytes
gold_answer: ingest.module_size_limit_bytes defaults to 262144 (256 KiB). Modules larger than this are split or skipped during ingest.
rephrasings: maximum module size accepted by cortex ingest

q: what function builds the recall pipeline
gold_answer: buildRecallPipeline in cmd/cortex/recall.go constructs a recall.Pipeline from live adapters (Neo4j Bolt, Ollama, Weaviate) and opens the segment writer used for reinforcement datoms.
rephrasings: where is the recall.Pipeline assembled in the CLI

q: what type does cortex use to represent recall output
gold_answer: recall.Response contains Results ([]Result) and ReinforcementDatoms ([]datom.Datom). Each Result carries EntryID, Body, Score, BaseActivation, PPRScore, Similarity, Importance, TrailContext, CommunityContext, and WhySurfaced.
rephrasings: fields of recall.Response

q: what is the config key for Ollama endpoint
gold_answer: endpoints.ollama defaults to localhost:11434.
rephrasings: default ollama url for cortex

q: what does cortex retract do
gold_answer: cortex retract appends a retraction datom for a prior assertion so the effective state excludes it going forward. The original datom stays in the log for replay and history.
rephrasings: purpose of the retract subcommand

q: what is the visibility threshold config key
gold_answer: retrieval.forgetting.visibility_threshold defaults to 0.05. Entries with composite activation below this are hidden from recall.
rephrasings: name of the forgetting threshold setting

q: what ingest strategy does cortex use for Go code
gold_answer: ingest.default_strategy.go=per-package — Go modules are ingested one unit per package.
rephrasings: default cortex ingest unit for Go files

q: how does cortex recall reinforce entries and where is the log write performed
gold_answer: recall.Pipeline.Recall returns unsealed ReinforcementDatoms but does not write them. The CLI (cmd/cortex/recall.go appendReinforcementDatoms) seals each datom and appends them as one transaction group to the log Writer opened by the CLI, preserving the pipeline-never-opens-its-own-log-handle invariant.
rephrasings: who writes the reinforcement datoms produced by recall
how is activation reinforcement persisted after a recall

q: how is concept extraction wired from config to the ollama client during recall
gold_answer: buildRecallPipeline builds an ollama client from Config.Endpoints.Ollama and wraps it in ollamaConceptExtractor, which is assigned to recall.Pipeline.Concepts. The pipeline calls Concepts.Extract(query) which issues an Ollama completion using the concept-extraction prompt from internal/prompts.
rephrasings: trace how a query gets concept-extracted in cortex

q: how does ingest push entries into both neo4j and weaviate
gold_answer: internal/ingest drives the pipeline: modules are summarized, embeddings are produced via Ollama, graph structure is written to Neo4j, and vectors + metadata are written to Weaviate. Every side-effect is preceded by a sealed datom append so replay can rebuild state.
rephrasings: which components write to which backend during ingest

q: how does the CLI surface operational errors from the pipeline
gold_answer: Pipeline code wraps underlying causes in errs.Operational/errs.Validation. The CLI's emitAndExit renders these with their cause chain and returns the cli.exit_code.operational / validation exit codes.
rephrasings: how are underlying errors shown to the user

q: how does PPR get its seeds from concept extraction output
gold_answer: Concepts from the extractor are passed to SeedResolver.Resolve(concepts, topK) — a neo4jSeedResolver that queries Neo4j for the top-k entries matching those concepts. Those entry ids then seed the PPRRunner.Run call.
rephrasings: how are concepts turned into seed node ids

q: how does cortex up bootstrap weaviate schema and which package owns it
gold_answer: cortex up (cmd/cortex/lifecycle.go) calls into internal/infra to start the docker stack, then into internal/weaviate to assert the required classes/schema exist. Schema ownership lives in internal/weaviate.
rephrasings: where is the weaviate schema created for a fresh cortex install

q: how does a failed ingest recover without losing prior modules
gold_answer: Every module ingest writes a sealed datom before it mutates derived state. On restart, self-heal replay (internal/replay) re-applies any datoms that were not reflected in Neo4j/Weaviate, and the RunInProgress marker tells the next ingest where to resume.
rephrasings: what guarantees partial ingest is idempotent

q: how does cortex handle real-time graphql subscriptions
gold_answer: Cortex has no graphql surface. It exposes a CLI and writes to Neo4j/Weaviate/Ollama; there is no subscription system, no graphql server, and no websocket push. Recall is a synchronous command invocation.
rephrasings: what is the graphql subscription model in cortex

q: how does cortex authenticate users via OAuth2
gold_answer: Cortex is a single-user local CLI. There is no OAuth2, no user model, and no auth middleware. The only credential in the system is the auto-generated Neo4j bolt password.
rephrasings: cortex oauth login flow

q: which react components render the cortex dashboard
gold_answer: Cortex has no react frontend. CORTEX_UI_SPEC.md may describe a planned UI but no components exist in the repo.
rephrasings: cortex frontend component tree

q: how does cortex shard writes across multiple kafka partitions
gold_answer: There is no kafka in cortex. All writes go to a single-process append-only segment log under ~/.cortex/log.d.
rephrasings: cortex kafka partitioning strategy

q: what is the cortex billing model for enterprise tenants
gold_answer: Cortex is a local developer tool with no billing, no tenancy, and no enterprise plan.
rephrasings: cortex pricing tiers
